question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers: Using the PICO model, the participants of concern were adults aged 18 years or older with type 1 or type 2 diabetes mellitus, cared for in any health-care setting, with an active foot ulcer of any stage. The intervention of interest was the delivery of DFU treatment with an MDT approach, whereupon, the team had to be multidisciplinary health professionals. The comparison of interest was the delivery of care to manage people with DFUs by an individual health professional or using an unstructured, noncollaborative care model. The primary outcome measures were amputation rates, ulcer development and wound healing. With regard to amputation rates this included the level of amputation. Secondary outcome measures were: patients' experiences, mortality and resource use, which included length of hospital stay and cost of care. All English-language studies employing the following designs were included: randomised control trials, controlled clinical trials and comparative pre and post studies.

question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria
answers: Using the PICO model, the participants of concern were adults aged 18 years or older with type 1 or type 2 diabetes mellitus, cared for in any health-care setting, with an active foot ulcer of any stage. The intervention of interest was the delivery of DFU treatment with an MDT approach, whereupon, the team had to be multidisciplinary health professionals. The comparison of interest was the delivery of care to manage people with DFUs by an individual health professional or using an unstructured, noncollaborative care model. The primary outcome measures were amputation rates, ulcer development and wound healing. With regard to amputation rates this included the level of amputation. Secondary outcome measures were: patients' experiences, mortality and resource use, which included length of hospital stay and cost of care. All English-language studies employing the following designs were included: randomised control trials, controlled clinical trials and comparative pre and post studies. Search strategy The following databases were searched over a period from 19 January 2016 to 21 January 2016: Cumulative Index to Nursing and Allied Health Literature (CINAHL), Medline, Embase and Cochrane Library. The following search terms were used: diabetic foot, multidisciplinary team, patient care team, multidisciplinary care team. The variation in terms was determined by the MeSH headings for each individual database. No limit was placed on year of publication. A limitation of English language only was placed on each search. The reference lists of chosen articles were screened but no additional articles were obtained from this screening.

question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers: No limit was placed on year of publication. A limitation of English language only was placed on each search. The reference lists of chosen articles were screened but no additional articles were obtained from this screening.

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers:
The following databases were searched over a period from 19 January 2016 to 21 January 2016: Cumulative Index to Nursing and Allied Health Literature (CINAHL),
Medline, Embase and Cochrane Library.

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers: The reference lists of chosen articles were screened but no additional articles were obtained from this screening

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers: nan

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers: Restrictions are inappropriate as it restricted english studies

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers: Potentially eligible studies were selected by screening titles and abstracts, independently, by two reviewers. Full copies of potentially eligible studies were then obtained and read. Articles were then selected independently, by two reviewers, by applying the predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers: Further articles were excluded at the data-extraction stage if they did not report on the outcomes required. The data from the included articles were extracted by two reviewers. The information extracted consisted of the first author, study title, year of publication, country where study was carried out, study design, care setting, sample size and findings.

question: Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
answers: Participants and sample size: All participants had a diagnosis of diabetes with complications affecting the feet. This included past and/or present foot ulceration. Interventions and follow-up All studies evaluated the effect of an MDT on diabetic foot care compared with no MDT.

question: Were all relevant study results collected for use in the synthesis?
answers:

Table 3. EBL checklist results

question: Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
answers: Quality appraisal was undertaken using the EBL Critical Appraisal checklist.18 For systematic reviews the EBL checklist was not suitable so the PRISMA 2009 Checklist was employed.19 Each stage of the research process was validated, independently, by a second reviewer. In the case of disagreement, the plan was for consensus to be reached through discussion.

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers: Table 2. Makeup of multidisciplinary teams (MDTs)

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers: nan

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers: Where appropriate, a meta-analysis was undertaken of the results. Where meta-analysis was not possible, a narrative synthesis of the studies was undertaken consisting of a summary of each study including an outline of the author, sample size, results and a synopsis of these results.

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
Within this systematic review, many of the study's results were reviewed individually rather than through a meta-analysis. This was due to a substantial heterogeneity in many cases. Meta-analysis can only be performed when studies are similar to each other.57

Meta-analysis could not be undertaken for total amputation rates due to a substantial heterogeneity (I-squared=69%, p=0.007). If I-squared is >30%, or there is a low p-value (<0.10) in
the chi-squared test for heterogeneity then it is not appropriate to undertake meta-analysis.50 Therefore total amputation rates had to be analysed separately with a combination of statistical and narrative analyses.

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
answers: nan

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers: nan

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
Much of the data were collected by analysing patients' notes or databases, retrospectively.31,32,35-39,42,46,48 This must be recognised as a limitation of these studies as they were relying on the relevant documentation for analysis having been completed accurately. Furthermore, there is a risk that all amputations recorded may not be done so in a way that allows for
a collective analysis. This was obvious in some of the studies, given the substantial heterogeneity noted when assessing suitability for meta-analysis.


Quality of the evidence
Although improvements were seen in many of the
outcomes post MDT care these results should be
interpreted with caution. Following quality appraisal,
61% of studies included were found not to reach the
required validity scores. This arose mainly due to
insufficient reporting within the studies, making it many
aspects of the published studies unclear. For example,
many of the studies failed to give clear sample sizes. This
left it impossible to interpret the results properly or to
perform further statistical analysis. A further concerning
point is that only one study declared that the researchers
played no part in care delivery. If the researchers in any
of the studies were also members of the MDT this would
introduce a strong element of bias to the studies.18
Within this systematic review, many of the study's
results were reviewed individually rather than through
a meta-analysis. This was due to a substantial
heterogeneity in many cases. Meta-analysis can only be
performed when studies are similar to each other.57
Furthermore, as the majority of studies were based in a
hospital setting and no one study had the exact same
team makeup it is difficult to generalise the results into
the wider clinical setting. The evidence presented
cannot be used to firmly guide future practice. From the
currently available evidence, an impact of the MDT on
diabetic foot outcomes can be seen but due to the lack
of high-quality evidence and substantial heterogeneity
these results should be interpreted with caution.
